speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: Gloucester, ’tis true that we are in great danger.

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: The greater therefore should our courage be.—

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: Good morrow, brother Bedford. God almighty,

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: There is some soul of goodness in things evil,

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: Would men observingly distill it out.

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: For our bad neighbor makes us early stirrers,

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: Which is both healthful and good husbandry.

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: Besides, they are our outward consciences

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: And preachers to us all, admonishing

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: That we should dress us fairly for our end.

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: Thus may we gather honey from the weed

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: And make a moral of the devil himself.

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: Good morrow, old Sir Thomas Erpingham.

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: A good soft pillow for that good white head

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: Were better than a churlish turf of France.

speaker: ERPINGHAM
text: Not so, my liege, this lodging likes me better,

speaker: ERPINGHAM
text: Since I may say “Now lie I like a king.”

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: ’Tis good for men to love their present pains

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: Upon example. So the spirit is eased;

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: And when the mind is quickened, out of doubt,

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: The organs, though defunct and dead before,

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: Break up their drowsy grave and newly move

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: With casted slough and fresh legerity.

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: Lend me thy cloak, Sir Thomas.

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: Brothers both,

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: Commend me to the princes in our camp,

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: Do my good morrow to them, and anon

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: Desire them all to my pavilion.

speaker: GLOUCESTER
text: We shall, my liege.

speaker: ERPINGHAM
text: Shall I attend your Grace?

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: No, my good knight.

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: Go with my brothers to my lords of England.

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: I and my bosom must debate awhile,

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: And then I would no other company.

speaker: ERPINGHAM
text: The Lord in heaven bless thee, noble Harry.

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: God-a-mercy, old heart, thou speak’st cheerfully.

speaker: PISTOL
text: Qui vous là?

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: A friend.

speaker: PISTOL
text: Discuss unto me: art thou officer or art thou

speaker: PISTOL
text: base, common, and popular?

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: I am a gentleman of a company.

speaker: PISTOL
text: Trail’st thou the puissant pike?

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: Even so. What are you?

speaker: PISTOL
text: As good a gentleman as the Emperor.

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: Then you are a better than the King.

speaker: PISTOL
text: The King’s a bawcock and a heart of gold, a lad

speaker: PISTOL
text: of life, an imp of fame, of parents good, of fist most

speaker: PISTOL
text: valiant. I kiss his dirty shoe, and from heartstring I

speaker: PISTOL
text: love the lovely bully. What is thy name?

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: Harry le Roy.

speaker: PISTOL
text: Le Roy? A Cornish name. Art thou of Cornish

speaker: PISTOL
text: crew?

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: No, I am a Welshman.

speaker: PISTOL
text: Know’st thou Fluellen?

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: Yes.

speaker: PISTOL
text: Tell him I’ll knock his leek about his pate upon

speaker: PISTOL
text: Saint Davy’s day.

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: Do not you wear your dagger in your cap

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: that day, lest he knock that about yours.

speaker: PISTOL
text: Art thou his friend?

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: And his kinsman too.

speaker: PISTOL
text: The figo for thee then!

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: I thank you. God be with you.

speaker: PISTOL
text: My name is Pistol called.

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: It sorts well with your fierceness.

speaker: GOWER
text: Captain Fluellen.

speaker: FLUELLEN
text: ’So. In the name of Jesu Christ, speak fewer.

speaker: FLUELLEN
text: It is the greatest admiration in the universal world

speaker: FLUELLEN
text: when the true and aunchient prerogatifes and

speaker: FLUELLEN
text: laws of the wars is not kept. If you would take the

speaker: FLUELLEN
text: pains but to examine the wars of Pompey the

speaker: FLUELLEN
text: Great, you shall find, I warrant you, that there is

speaker: FLUELLEN
text: no tiddle taddle nor pibble babble in Pompey’s

speaker: FLUELLEN
text: camp. I warrant you, you shall find the ceremonies

speaker: FLUELLEN
text: of the wars and the cares of it and the forms

speaker: FLUELLEN
text: of it and the sobriety of it and the modesty of it to

speaker: FLUELLEN
text: be otherwise.

speaker: GOWER
text: Why, the enemy is loud. You hear him all

speaker: GOWER
text: night.

speaker: FLUELLEN
text: If the enemy is an ass and a fool and a prating

speaker: FLUELLEN
text: coxcomb, is it meet, think you, that we should also,

speaker: FLUELLEN
text: look you, be an ass and a fool and a prating

speaker: FLUELLEN
text: coxcomb, in your own conscience now?

speaker: GOWER
text: I will speak lower.

speaker: FLUELLEN
text: I pray you and beseech you that you will.

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: Though it appear a little out of fashion,

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: There is much care and valor in this Welshman.

speaker: COURT
text: Brother John Bates, is not that the morning

speaker: COURT
text: which breaks yonder?

speaker: BATES
text: I think it be, but we have no great cause to desire

speaker: BATES
text: the approach of day.

speaker: WILLIAMS
text: We see yonder the beginning of the day, but

speaker: WILLIAMS
text: I think we shall never see the end of it.—Who goes

speaker: WILLIAMS
text: there?

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: A friend.

speaker: WILLIAMS
text: Under what captain serve you?

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: Under Sir Thomas Erpingham.

speaker: WILLIAMS
text: A good old commander and a most kind

speaker: WILLIAMS
text: gentleman. I pray you, what thinks he of our

speaker: WILLIAMS
text: estate?

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: Even as men wracked upon a sand, that

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: look to be washed off the next tide.

speaker: BATES
text: He hath not told his thought to the King?

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: No. Nor it is not meet he should, for,

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: though I speak it to you, I think the King is but a

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: man as I am. The violet smells to him as it doth to

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: me. The element shows to him as it doth to me. All

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: his senses have but human conditions. His ceremonies

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: laid by, in his nakedness he appears but a man,

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: and though his affections are higher mounted than

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: ours, yet when they stoop, they stoop with the like

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: wing. Therefore, when he sees reason of fears as we

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: do, his fears, out of doubt, be of the same relish as

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: ours are. Yet, in reason, no man should possess him

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: with any appearance of fear, lest he, by showing it,

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: should dishearten his army.

speaker: BATES
text: He may show what outward courage he will,

speaker: BATES
text: but I believe, as cold a night as ’tis, he could wish

speaker: BATES
text: himself in Thames up to the neck; and so I would

speaker: BATES
text: he were, and I by him, at all adventures, so we were

speaker: BATES
text: quit here.

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: By my troth, I will speak my conscience

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: of the King. I think he would not wish himself

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: anywhere but where he is.

speaker: BATES
text: Then I would he were here alone; so should he

speaker: BATES
text: be sure to be ransomed, and a many poor men’s

speaker: BATES
text: lives saved.

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: I dare say you love him not so ill to wish

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: him here alone, howsoever you speak this to feel

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: other men’s minds. Methinks I could not die anywhere

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: so contented as in the King’s company, his

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: cause being just and his quarrel honorable.

speaker: WILLIAMS
text: That’s more than we know.

speaker: BATES
text: Ay, or more than we should seek after, for we

speaker: BATES
text: know enough if we know we are the King’s subjects.

speaker: BATES
text: If his cause be wrong, our obedience to the

speaker: BATES
text: King wipes the crime of it out of us.

speaker: WILLIAMS
text: But if the cause be not good, the King

speaker: WILLIAMS
text: himself hath a heavy reckoning to make, when all

speaker: WILLIAMS
text: those legs and arms and heads, chopped off in a

speaker: WILLIAMS
text: battle, shall join together at the latter day, and cry

speaker: WILLIAMS
text: all “We died at such a place,” some swearing, some

speaker: WILLIAMS
text: crying for a surgeon, some upon their wives left

speaker: WILLIAMS
text: poor behind them, some upon the debts they owe,

speaker: WILLIAMS
text: some upon their children rawly left. I am afeard

speaker: WILLIAMS
text: there are few die well that die in a battle, for how

speaker: WILLIAMS
text: can they charitably dispose of anything when blood

speaker: WILLIAMS
text: is their argument? Now, if these men do not die

speaker: WILLIAMS
text: well, it will be a black matter for the king that led

speaker: WILLIAMS
text: them to it, who to disobey were against all proportion

speaker: WILLIAMS
text: of subjection.

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: So, if a son that is by his father sent about

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: merchandise do sinfully miscarry upon the sea,

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: the imputation of his wickedness, by your rule,

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: should be imposed upon his father that sent him.

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: Or if a servant, under his master’s command transporting

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: a sum of money, be assailed by robbers and

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: die in many irreconciled iniquities, you may call the

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: business of the master the author of the servant’s

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: damnation. But this is not so. The King is not bound

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: to answer the particular endings of his soldiers, the

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: father of his son, nor the master of his servant, for

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: they purpose not their death when they purpose

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: their services. Besides, there is no king, be his cause

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: never so spotless, if it come to the arbitrament of

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: swords, can try it out with all unspotted soldiers.

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: Some, peradventure, have on them the guilt of

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: premeditated and contrived murder; some, of beguiling

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: virgins with the broken seals of perjury;

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: some, making the wars their bulwark, that have

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: before gored the gentle bosom of peace with pillage

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: and robbery. Now, if these men have defeated the

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: law and outrun native punishment, though they can

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: outstrip men, they have no wings to fly from God.

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: War is His beadle, war is His vengeance, so that here

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: men are punished for before-breach of the King’s

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: laws in now the King’s quarrel. Where they feared

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: the death, they have borne life away; and where they

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: would be safe, they perish. Then, if they die unprovided,

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: no more is the King guilty of their damnation

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: than he was before guilty of those impieties for the

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: which they are now visited. Every subject’s duty is

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: the King’s, but every subject’s soul is his own.

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: Therefore should every soldier in the wars do as

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: every sick man in his bed: wash every mote out of

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: his conscience. And, dying so, death is to him

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: advantage; or not dying, the time was blessedly lost

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: wherein such preparation was gained. And in him

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: that escapes, it were not sin to think that, making

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: God so free an offer, He let him outlive that day to

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: see His greatness and to teach others how they

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: should prepare.

speaker: WILLIAMS
text: ’Tis certain, every man that dies ill, the ill

speaker: WILLIAMS
text: upon his own head; the King is not to answer it.

speaker: BATES
text: I do not desire he should answer for me, and yet

speaker: BATES
text: I determine to fight lustily for him.

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: I myself heard the King say he would not

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: be ransomed.

speaker: WILLIAMS
text: Ay, he said so to make us fight cheerfully,

speaker: WILLIAMS
text: but when our throats are cut, he may be ransomed

speaker: WILLIAMS
text: and we ne’er the wiser.

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: If I live to see it, I will never trust his

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: word after.

speaker: WILLIAMS
text: You pay him then. That’s a perilous shot out

speaker: WILLIAMS
text: of an elder gun, that a poor and a private displeasure

speaker: WILLIAMS
text: can do against a monarch. You may as well go

speaker: WILLIAMS
text: about to turn the sun to ice with fanning in his face

speaker: WILLIAMS
text: with a peacock’s feather. You’ll “never trust his

speaker: WILLIAMS
text: word after.” Come, ’tis a foolish saying.

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: Your reproof is something too round. I

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: should be angry with you if the time were

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: convenient.

speaker: WILLIAMS
text: Let it be a quarrel between us, if you live.

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: I embrace it.

speaker: WILLIAMS
text: How shall I know thee again?

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: Give me any gage of thine, and I will wear

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: it in my bonnet. Then, if ever thou dar’st acknowledge

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: it, I will make it my quarrel.

speaker: WILLIAMS
text: Here’s my glove. Give me another of thine.

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: There.

speaker: WILLIAMS
text: This will I also wear in my cap. If ever thou

speaker: WILLIAMS
text: come to me and say, after tomorrow, “This is my

speaker: WILLIAMS
text: glove,” by this hand I will take thee a box on the

speaker: WILLIAMS
text: ear.

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: If ever I live to see it, I will challenge it.

speaker: WILLIAMS
text: Thou dar’st as well be hanged.

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: Well, I will do it, though I take thee in the

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: King’s company.

speaker: WILLIAMS
text: Keep thy word. Fare thee well.

speaker: BATES
text: Be friends, you English fools, be friends. We

speaker: BATES
text: have French quarrels enough, if you could tell how

speaker: BATES
text: to reckon.

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: Indeed, the French may lay twenty

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: French crowns to one they will beat us, for they

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: bear them on their shoulders. But it is no English

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: treason to cut French crowns, and tomorrow the

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: King himself will be a clipper.

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: Upon the King! Let us our lives, our souls, our

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: debts, our careful wives, our children, and our sins,

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: lay on the King!

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: We must bear all. O hard condition,

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: Twin-born with greatness, subject to the breath

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: Of every fool whose sense no more can feel

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: But his own wringing. What infinite heart’s ease

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: Must kings neglect that private men enjoy?

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: And what have kings that privates have not too,

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: Save ceremony, save general ceremony?

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: And what art thou, thou idol ceremony?

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: What kind of god art thou that suffer’st more

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: Of mortal griefs than do thy worshipers?

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: What are thy rents? What are thy comings-in?

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: O ceremony, show me but thy worth!

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: What is thy soul of adoration?

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: Art thou aught else but place, degree, and form,

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: Creating awe and fear in other men,

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: Wherein thou art less happy, being feared,

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: Than they in fearing?

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: What drink’st thou oft, instead of homage sweet,

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: But poisoned flattery? O, be sick, great greatness,

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: And bid thy ceremony give thee cure!

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: Think’st thou the fiery fever will go out

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: With titles blown from adulation?

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: Will it give place to flexure and low bending?

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: Canst thou, when thou command’st the beggar’s knee,

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: Command the health of it? No, thou proud dream,

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: That play’st so subtly with a king’s repose.

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: I am a king that find thee, and I know

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: ’Tis not the balm, the scepter, and the ball,

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: The sword, the mace, the crown imperial,

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: The intertissued robe of gold and pearl,

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: The farcèd title running ’fore the King,

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: The throne he sits on, nor the tide of pomp

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: That beats upon the high shore of this world;

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: No, not all these, thrice-gorgeous ceremony,

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: Not all these, laid in bed majestical,

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: Can sleep so soundly as the wretched slave

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: Who, with a body filled and vacant mind,

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: Gets him to rest, crammed with distressful bread;

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: Never sees horrid night, the child of hell,

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: But, like a lackey, from the rise to set

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: Sweats in the eye of Phoebus, and all night

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: Sleeps in Elysium; next day after dawn

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: Doth rise and help Hyperion to his horse,

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: And follows so the ever-running year

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: With profitable labor to his grave.

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: And, but for ceremony, such a wretch,

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: Winding up days with toil and nights with sleep,

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: Had the forehand and vantage of a king.

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: The slave, a member of the country’s peace,

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: Enjoys it, but in gross brain little wots

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: What watch the King keeps to maintain the peace,

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: Whose hours the peasant best advantages.

speaker: ERPINGHAM
text: My lord, your nobles, jealous of your absence,

speaker: ERPINGHAM
text: Seek through your camp to find you.

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: Good old knight,

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: Collect them all together at my tent.

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: I’ll be before thee.

speaker: ERPINGHAM
text: I shall do ’t, my lord.

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: O God of battles, steel my soldiers’ hearts.

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: Possess them not with fear. Take from them now

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: The sense of reck’ning or th’ opposèd numbers

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: Pluck their hearts from them. Not today, O Lord,

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: O, not today, think not upon the fault

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: My father made in compassing the crown.

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: I Richard’s body have interrèd new

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: And on it have bestowed more contrite tears

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: Than from it issued forcèd drops of blood.

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: Five hundred poor I have in yearly pay

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: Who twice a day their withered hands hold up

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: Toward heaven to pardon blood. And I have built

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: Two chantries where the sad and solemn priests

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: Sing still for Richard’s soul. More will I do—

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: Though all that I can do is nothing worth,

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: Since that my penitence comes after all,

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: Imploring pardon.

speaker: GLOUCESTER
text: My liege.

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: My brother Gloucester’s voice.—Ay,

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: I know thy errand. I will go with thee.

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: The day, my friends, and all things stay for me.
